access.accessMethod: Contact Archives and Manuscript Collections to arrange access; the catalog says physical access is restricted and a digital surrogate is available.
access.entityLabel: National Library of Scotland, Advocates MS 19.2.1 (Auchinleck manuscript)

entities.description: The principal codex from which installments I–II and IV–IX draw discontiguous texts. Installment III instead uses leaves detached from this codex and now held at Edinburgh University Library.

holding.notes: The current codex contains i–viii + 334 + ix–xiv folios plus modern endleaves; ten detached folios survive in other repositories.

entities.label: National Library of Scotland, Advocates MS 19.2.1 (Auchinleck manuscript)

physicalDescription.extent: i–viii + 334 + ix–xiv folios, 14 stubs, modern endleaves, and 10 detached folios in other repositories
notes: Probably produced in London; the current codex is incomplete and many leaves or miniatures are lost.

rights.basis: The anonymous texts and this fourteenth-century witness predate modern copyright terms by centuries.
rights.component: Anonymous medieval textual content represented by the Auchinleck witness
rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions with ordinary author-term copyright limits
notes: This does not cover modern editions, transcriptions, photographs, descriptions, or interface assets.

access.accessMethod: Consult the open collection in the Centre for Research Collections, Edinburgh University Main Library.
access.entityLabel: University of Edinburgh Library MS 218, Auchinleck fragments
access.provider: University of Edinburgh Library Heritage Collections

entities.description: Four detached Auchinleck leaves; the second bifolium, ff. 3–4, carries the two King Richard fragments transcribed by Kölbing in installment III.

holding.notes: Two leaves carry Life of Adam and Eve and two carry Richard Coer de Lyon.

entities.label: University of Edinburgh Library MS 218, Auchinleck fragments

notes: Double columns, 44 lines per page; formerly used as binding waste.

access.accessMethod: Registered users may consult Special Collections; remote researchers may request a virtual reading-room appointment or enquire about ordered images.
access.format: Physical manuscript or mediated digital images

entities.description: A mid-fifteenth-century paper household manuscript. Kölbing based the later version of A Peniworth of Witt on its How a Merchant Betrayed His Wife at ff. 57v–59r.

holding.notes: No repository-owned exact item record was located in the public search; the exact-content handlist is scholarly rather than substituted as an institutional catalog.

identifiers.scheme: DIMEV/IMEV for How a merchant betrayed his wife

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Ff.2.38

physicalDescription.extent: iii + 247 + i leaves in its present state
notes: Two scribes; current foliation accounts for lost material and later rebinding.

access.accessMethod: Request the physical item through the British Library collection-item request route; consultation is in London and requires a current Reader Pass.
No complete public digital facsimile was found in the current exact catalog or digitized-manuscript listings.

dateStatements.label: Middle English verse unit
dateStatements.value: mid fifteenth century; date 1456 occurs on f. 306

entities.description: A composite miscellany whose mid-fifteenth-century Middle English verse unit is ff. 271–311v. Kölbing collated the first 167 lines of its A Penyworth of Wytt text from f. 286 onward against Cambridge Ff.2.38.

holding.notes: The exact poem is identified by Kölbing at f. 286 onward; the current catalog describes the enclosing verse collection rather than itemizing this poem.

entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 5396

physicalDescription.extent: 311 folios; Middle English verse unit ff. 271–311v
notes: Composite of four codicological units.

entities.description: The Vernon version of The King of Tars at ff. 304vb–307ra, printed by Krause in parallel with Auchinleck.

holding.notes: Digital Bodleian supplies a complete 710-canvas object including covers and spine.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1 (Vernon Manuscript)

physicalDescription.extent: viii + 342 + i folios, incomplete; originally viii + 413
notes: Produced in England, probably the West Midlands; Middle English religious and didactic compilation with over 350 items.

access.accessMethod: Request the physical item through the British Library collection-item request route; consultation is in London and requires a current Reader Pass.

entities.description: The third King of Tars witness, ff. 126rc–128va. Krause treated it as a close copy of Vernon and recorded its minor variants beneath the Vernon column.

holding.notes: Current exact component description identifies The King of Tars at ff. 126r(c)–128v(a).

entities.label: British Library, Add MS 22283 (Simeon Manuscript)

notes: Middle English, Middle French, and Latin miscellany associated with the Vernon manuscript; probably produced in the West Midlands.

access.accessMethod: Browse project transcriptions and open exact public IIIF Image API surfaces.
access.format: image/jpeg and application/json bundled as ZIP

localCopies.label: Complete public IIIF image-inventory bundle for the Auchinleck manuscript and detached fragments
notes: The verified 701-image list was enumerated from all manuscript-item facsimile links in the provider's complete Auchinleck project; the provider exposes Image API services but no Presentation manifest.
ZIP contains the normalized exact source URL list, inventory.json, and every image in that list at the provider resource profile.
Coverage is complete for the public project inventory and includes detached fragments; it does not assert that covers, spine, edges, or preservation-master files are exposed.
Caching records a research copy only and does not override the project's publication and commercial-use restrictions.

workPortion.label: CME00004's discontiguous Auchinleck texts and Edinburgh MS 218 King Richard fragments
locators: IX The King of Tars: ff. 7ra–13vb; inventory items 13–26
V On the Seven Deadly Sins, VI Paternoster, and IV Assumption: ff. 70ra–78ra around damaged f. 72A; inventory items 143–161
II A Peniworþ of Witt: f. 256A stub–259rb; project inventory items 523–530 include the supplemental 256 surfaces through f. 259r
VII Dauid þe King: ff. 280rb–280vb; inventory items 574–575
VIII The Four Foes of Mankind: f. 303rb–303vb; inventory items 622–623
I Alphabetical Praise of Women: ff. 324ra–325vb; inventory items 664–667
III King Richard: Edinburgh University Library MS 218 fragment 2, ff. 3ra–4vb; inventory items 690–693
notes: The start/end links bound the earliest and latest main-codex ranges only; they do not imply that all intervening leaves belong to CME00004.
The King Richard source is the detached Edinburgh fragment, not the main-codex ff. 326–327 or St Andrews fragments also present in the full project.
The official catalog's uncertainty around the damaged f. 72A boundary is retained rather than converted into a false precise folio assignment.

rights.basis: The project's Copyright and use statement says all site materials are copyrighted, permits personal use, and prohibits unauthorized commercial use or publication.
rights.component: Project texts, essays, manuscript images, descriptions, and reference material
notes: Public image access and local caching do not authorize redistribution or commercial publication.

entities.description: The NLS-supported project exposes a full transcription and public facsimile links for 701 surviving codex and detached-fragment surfaces through IIIF Image API services. No Presentation manifest is offered, so the complete verified URL inventory is bundled locally.
entities.label: Auchinleck Manuscript project digital facsimile

physicalDescription.extent: 701 public leaf, stub, and detached-fragment images in the verified project inventory
notes: Representative inspection includes ordinary pages, rubricated leaves, and the painted King of Tars opening; color and gold/red/blue decoration are visibly preserved.

rights.basis: The project's Copyright and use statement says all site materials are copyrighted, permits personal use, and prohibits unauthorized commercial use or publication.
rights.component: Project texts, essays, manuscript images, descriptions, and reference material
notes: Public image access and local caching do not authorize redistribution or commercial publication.

entities.description: A printed whole-manuscript facsimile introduced by Frances McSparran and P. R. Robinson. No complete public digital copy was verified.

notes: Color/fidelity characteristics were not verified from a copy.

access.accessMethod: Browse Digital Bodleian or open the exact IIIF Presentation manifest and canvas images.
access.entityLabel: Digital Bodleian complete IIIF facsimile of the Vernon Manuscript
access.format: IIIF Presentation 2 JSON and JPEG images

localCopies.label: Complete Vernon Manuscript IIIF bundle
notes: ZIP retains the exact provider manifest, inventory.json, and all 710 canvas images requested at 1600-pixel width.
The full physical-source digital object runs from upper cover through spine/lower cover; coverage does not imply preservation-master resolution.
Caching does not remove the manifest's CC BY-NC 4.0 attribution and noncommercial condition.

workPortion.label: The King of Tars, Vernon version
notes: The text begins in column b of fol. 304v and ends in column a of fol. 307r; both bounding folio images are retained in full.

localCopies.label: Exact Vernon Manuscript IIIF Presentation manifest
notes: The manifest alone is metadata; the separate ZIP is the complete cached image bundle.

rights.basis: The exact IIIF manifest attribution states Photo © Bodleian Libraries and identifies CC BY-NC 4.0 terms of use.
rights.component: Digital Bodleian manuscript photographs
rights.jurisdiction: Worldwide license grant, subject to license terms
notes: The license permits reuse only under its attribution and noncommercial conditions; it does not become unrestricted because the manuscript text is medieval.

entities.description: A 710-canvas digital object spanning the upper cover through spine/lower cover. The King of Tars occupies six canvases, fol. 304v through fol. 307r.
entities.label: Digital Bodleian complete IIIF facsimile of the Vernon Manuscript

notes: Representative inspection of fols. 304v and 307r shows the three-column text, colored initials, rubrication, and ornamental borders in color.

rights.basis: The exact IIIF manifest attribution states Photo © Bodleian Libraries and identifies CC BY-NC 4.0 terms of use.

rights.jurisdiction: Worldwide license grant, subject to license terms

next_steps: Ask NLS whether Adv.MS.19.2.1D has an exportable complete-object file or Presentation manifest.
If additional binding surfaces are exposed, cache the complete physical-object sequence and retain the present project bundle as a separately identified transcription-linked facsimile.
notes: The current bundle is complete for the project's verified 701 public leaf/fragment surfaces and already includes every CME00004 Auchinleck surface.
openQuestions.question: Does the current NLS permanent digital object expose covers, spine, endleaves, and other binding surfaces beyond the 701-image Auchinleck project inventory, and is a direct whole-object download available?

next_steps: Ask Cambridge Special Collections or the Cultural Heritage Imaging Laboratory about a whole-codex digital order and reuse terms.
Consult the complete 1979 Scolar Press facsimile through a holding library in the meantime.
notes: No complete openly downloadable digital facsimile was found in CUDL, Internet Archive, Google Books, or the exact scholarly handlist during this review.
openQuestions.question: Can Cambridge University Library supply or authorize a complete digital facsimile of MS Ff.2.38, rather than only mediated viewing or individual ordered images?

next_steps: Ask the British Library for current digitization and whole-manuscript reproduction options, citing Harley MS 5396 and the poem beginning at f. 286.
If only selected images can be supplied, mark their coverage partial and do not describe them as the whole codex.
openQuestions.question: Has British Library Harley MS 5396 been digitized or can a complete facsimile be ordered?

next_steps: Monitor the exact Archives and Manuscripts record and the Library's Digitised Manuscripts recovery page.
Ask the British Library whether a mediated full-manuscript copy can be supplied while the viewer remains offline.
notes: The catalog establishes that full digital coverage exists but currently marks the images unavailable; no access controls were bypassed.
openQuestions.question: When will the British Library restore public access to the complete Add MS 22283 digital facsimile, and under what item-level image terms?

lineage.summary: CME00004 is a selective U-M electronic compilation of six articles published as nine numbered installments in Englische Studien 7–11 (1884–1888). The articles edit several discontiguous portions of the Auchinleck manuscript and its detached Edinburgh fragments; installment II also prints Cambridge Ff.2.38 with Harley 5396 variants, and installment IX prints Auchinleck and Vernon in parallel with Simeon variants. Complete public journal volumes and the complete exposed Auchinleck and Vernon facsimile sets are cached locally, with exact work portions recorded inside the larger objects.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English authors
catalogSummary.date: Medieval texts attested from c. 1331–1340 onward; source installments first published 1884–1888
catalogSummary.editor: Eugen Kölbing; Max Schwarz; F. Krause
catalogSummary.region: England; exact medieval composition places vary or remain unresolved
catalogSummary.title: Kleine Publicationen aus der Auchinleck-Hs. I–IX

catalogingSubject.label: Kleine Publicationen aus der Auchinleck-Hs. I–IX
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The edition-level selection represented by University of Michigan CME item CME00004: nine numbered installments first published as six articles in Englische Studien 7–11 (1884–1888). It contains anonymous Middle English verse texts and versions edited by Eugen Kölbing, Max Schwarz, and F. Krause, plus surviving editorial headings, notes, and afterword matter. This record does not collapse the nine medieval works, their parallel versions, the journal articles, manuscript witnesses, U-M encoding, or repository XML into one abstract medieval work.

contentParts.label: I. Alphabetical Praise of Women (Lob der Frauen)

locators.value: article pp. 101–110; CME00004 begins at p. 103

notes: The poem begins imperfectly; its source and current catalog titles differ without identifying distinct works.
contentParts.summary: An incomplete praise-of-women poem, including praise grounded in the Virgin Mary, edited from the Auchinleck manuscript.

contentParts.label: II. A Peniworth of Witt / How a Merchant Betrayed His Wife

locators.value: fragmentary parallel text from f. 286 onward, lines 1–167

contentParts.summary: Two distinct Middle English versions of the merchant, wife, and mistress narrative: an incomplete-opening Auchinleck version and a later Cambridge version collated with a Harley fragment.

contentParts.label: III. Two Fragments of King Richard
contentParts.summary: Two separated fragments of the Auchinleck version of Richard Coer de Lyon, corresponding to Kölbing's line ranges 1745–1918 and 2579–2762 and now held as part of Edinburgh University Library MS 218.

contentParts.label: IV. The Assumption of the Blessed Virgin in Tail-Rhyme Stanzas
contentParts.summary: A Middle English Assumption legend in six-line tail-rhyme stanzas, edited by Max Schwarz from the Auchinleck witness.

contentParts.label: V. On the Seven Deadly Sins
contentParts.summary: A religious-didactic poem whose contents extend beyond its supplied title to confession, commandments, creed, Ave Maria, the Passion, prayer, and judgment.

contentParts.label: VI. The Paternoster
contentParts.summary: A Middle English exposition of the Lord's Prayer in verse.

contentParts.label: VII. Psalm 50 (Dauid þe King)
contentParts.summary: A stanzaic Middle English rendering and exposition of Psalm 50/51, structured by Latin psalm incipits.

contentParts.label: VIII. The Four Foes of Mankind
contentParts.summary: A northern Middle English religious poem on the world, flesh, devil, and death in alliterating sixteen-line stanzas.

contentParts.label: IX. The King of Tars
notes: The 2,357 XML L-element count includes four Vernon heading lines and is not a canonical combined line total.
contentParts.summary: Parallel edited texts of the incomplete Auchinleck and complete Vernon versions of the tail-rhyme romance, with variants from the closely related Simeon manuscript.

dateStatements.display: The six source articles appeared across Englische Studien 7 (1884), 8 (1885), 9 (1886), and 11 (1888)
notes: This is the publication range of the edition-level selection, not a medieval composition date.

dateStatements.display: The Auchinleck versions represented in every installment are witnessed in a manuscript produced c. 1331–1340; installment III uses detached leaves from the same codex
notes: This is witness production and first-known-attestation evidence. It does not assign all nine compositions to London in the 1330s.

extent.description: Nine numbered source installments contain twelve separately counted verse items or versions and are represented by ten top-level XML DIV1 elements: installment II's two versions are split into separate DIV1 elements, installment III comprises two fragments within one DIV1, and installment IX contains two version divisions. The repository XML has 5,159 L elements, 388 LG elements, 102 PB elements, and 484 NOTE elements. These are encoding metrics: L includes four heading lines in the Vernon King of Tars and does not establish canonical line totals; editorial prose occurs outside the verse-item count.

formStatements.description: All nine selected medieval textual units are verse; surviving German prose is editorial framing and apparatus.

languageStatements.label: German
notes: The medieval verse is not German; this language is confined to the edition layer.

scope.description: Article titles, introductions, notes, and other nineteenth-century editorial matter
Liturgical and biblical incipits or quotations embedded in Middle English verse

notes: All part-level line and stanza totals are XML representation metrics, not canonical work extents.
The U-M title's 1884–1889 date span is preserved as provider metadata; direct title-page inspection establishes 1888 for volume 11 and therefore 1884–1888 for the six source articles.
Manuscript image rights and access terms are recorded separately in the linked lineage manifest and do not follow from the public-domain status of the medieval texts.

next_steps: Consult current critical editions and DIMEV records for each component rather than transferring witness dates and dialects to composition.
Record competing scholarly ranges part by part where they are materially useful.
notes: The present record deliberately preserves the defensible edition and witness dates without filling this gap from expectation.
openQuestions.question: What composition dates and places can current specialist scholarship defend for each of the nine medieval textual units and their versions?

notes: This broad cultural origin does not turn London copying of the Auchinleck codex or West Midlands copying of Vernon/Simeon into places of composition for every text.
Whole-selection scope means that all included medieval texts are anonymous; it does not posit a single shared author.

summaries.value: A nineteenth-century serial edition of nine Middle English verse units: Alphabetical Praise of Women; two versions of A Peniworth of Witt/How a Merchant Betrayed His Wife; two Auchinleck fragments of King Richard; a tail-rhyme Assumption of the Virgin; On the Seven Deadly Sins; The Paternoster; a verse rendering of Psalm 50 called Dauid þe King; The Four Foes of Mankind; and parallel Auchinleck and Vernon texts of The King of Tars with Simeon variants.

CME00004 selects the edited Middle English texts and much of their local apparatus but omits substantial article introductions and discussions. It also serializes the two parallel King of Tars columns as consecutive XML version divisions, so its ten top-level DIV1 elements do not equal ten independent numbered installments.

notes: The roman-numeral range follows the recurring source-article title and preserves the edition-level boundary.
titles.value: Kleine Publicationen aus der Auchinleck-Hs. I–IX

notes: This is U-M's discovery title verbatim. The terminal year 1889 conflicts with the 1888 title page of Englische Studien 11 and is retained here as provider metadata rather than silently corrected.

Ueber die sieben todsunden. 6. Das Vater unser. 7. Psalm L. 8. Die feinde des menschen. 9. The king of Tars. [Englische Studien 7-11 (1884-1889)]
